speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number two. Interview with Abraham Jachsel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: December 1, 1994. OK. You can go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was asking you, before we left, that-- that-- if you could remember-- because I happen to know [NON-ENGLISH], an I love that. So I wanted to know if you could let history know a little bit of a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] It started with [NON-ENGLISH SINGING] And this was going down for an hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know? And it changed from one melody to the other to the other. And then on the end, you know, when were-- and then my mother, you know, and it's-- you know, everybody is so hungry. [NON-ENGLISH SINGING]

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's [NON-ENGLISH], all right? How about [NON-ENGLISH]? You remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I would not remember [NON-ENGLISH]. I will have to take a sit.

speaker: interviewer
text: The melody?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember the melody?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wouldn't remember. No-- I would-- I didn't hear so many years, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: But it affects you, you say, when you heard--

speaker: interviewee
text: It--it affects me. You know, I didn't like it. I was always the thing-- but whatever it is-- but let's say 10 years ago, me and Dave and another fellow, who was, you know, his wife doesn't know that he-- that even he's Jewish. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: We went to [NON-ENGLISH], and we went to Brooklyn. And we went to one rabbi after the other. We did the whole tour, you know? Completely the whole tour.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to the [NON-ENGLISH], and to the [NON-ENGLISH], and we went to the [NON-ENGLISH], and-- the whole works. And we got good and tired. And-- and good and drunk, too, you know. Every place they give you drinks and stuff like this. That was, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to switch to the organization that you said that you belonged to. Your father wanted you to belong to Bnei Akiva, which was more of an orthodox group, but you belong to Hashomer Hatzair. Tell me a little bit. I liked it better, Hashomer Hatzair. There was-- it was nice. It was free. And the world was-- we didn't know about politics. We were singing, and we went out on the-- how would you call this? We went by the--by the river over there, and we play soccer. And-- I remember on the-- Purim was nice. They dressed us up, and we were, as an organization, coming out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- they give a-- No, it's really nice. Purim in Krakow was like Purim in Tel Aviv. I mean, there were those big platform trucks-- oh, made of-- forget about the street car. Couldn't get through the street, and they would turn it over if they would even think about getting through. And music and-- I mean it, was really something. It was a happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: Purim.

speaker: interviewee
text: Purim. Purim was such a happening, and I heard that the only other place in which you could see that would be in Tel Aviv, in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with the Hashomer Hatzair.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that time. This was the only time where I was-- and what else would sting-- you know, like once a year, we had to stay on the corners, and give a little blue and white thing. And the people used to give you a nickel, a dime, a quarter. You know, and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was for Israel? The money was going to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Though this was for Keren Kayemeth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Keren Kayemeth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Keren Kayemeth was about the only-- the main organization what you-- you send money to. You know, I don't know, my mother couldn't go [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, it wouldn't put in the [NON-ENGLISH], you know. There was a [NON-ENGLISH] that was Keren Kayemeth-- [NON-ENGLISH]. This was the most important. Now, there was another one. Yes, there was a-- there was a charity for girls who don't have a [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't know how I explain to--

speaker: interviewer
text: A dowry.

speaker: interviewee
text: A dowry would be, yes. And then there was a-- this was a very important charity. And I remember once a big rabbi in [NON-ENGLISH]. I went to my aunt, and I had to go to the shul. And all my cousins are Hasidim, you know, and I was-- and he explained how important the charity is, you know. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So that the girls could get married. Because you can--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, you know, and for a kid-- 12, 13, 14 years, it's kind of funny, you know. [LAUGHS] It didn't get in. This has to cook a-- this has to take until you get all that to understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of your leaders in the Hashomer Hatzair? Were there-- were there teenagers? Were there adults?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were young people, mostly. But you know-- like you know, you stay-- you say the honor. I don't know, to [NON-ENGLISH] of that time. Right? And then there was-- who was the main from Hashomer Hatzair? What was very important? I don't remember the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your town. In Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in Krakow. I'm talking from the originators from Hatzair. No, this was the other one. Trumpeldorf? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know what, it's-- this is-- I'm not quite sure. But they-- Hashomer Hatzair was not a religious movement.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, not at all. Well, this was-- this was good for me. You know-- this was fight, you know? Most of it. But it was-- it was-- it was more [NON-ENGLISH], you know, more-- a little tough a little bit, let's say. And I-- and-- and [NON-ENGLISH] went in-- he went with me. He grabbed me by the thing, and on the solid, and I had to go all the way at the end of the street. And I see the kids, and they were-- I don't know. It wasn't my cup of coffee.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were more your type, Hashomer Hatzair.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hashomer Hatzair was more my type, you know. But it was kid's stuff. We didn't know politics, and they didn't teach us any politics. They didn't tell us-- it was so wonderful.

speaker: interviewer
text: You meet-- you meet a group of kids. You go swimming on Saturday. The other ones couldn't go Saturday swimming. You know? Did they teach you a love for Israel? Is that what was being done?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the main important thing. They were talking about, you know, the songs-- [NON-ENGLISH SINGING].

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, when I went to Israel, I wanted to see the Kibbutz, you know? And-- and the man who had to go show me, he has a big factory over there. And he say, look, you know, first come to see my place. I never got to it. Yeah. But that's about-- I'm going take you back a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: To September 6, 1939, when Krakow was occupied by the Germans. Do remember that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the day was Wednesday. The war started Friday. And-- I live about-- I used to live about let's say 1- blocks from the bridge, and they come-- the Germans came through the bridge. And we saw it like 15 minutes before. We couldn't understand, and I had to-- you know-- the-- the boys over there, they weren't even Jewish. When I hang around around the house, I say, something is wrong. They stopped bombing, you know. It's so quiet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then we saw the-- the priest, the cardinal. What was his name? With the-- with the horse and buggy, and nice, dressed up. And had a silver tray, and a bread, and water. And going to the bridge-- those--

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was the surrender. It's-- the city-- Krakow is a city like Rome or Paris. It's not supposed to be bombed. It's a historical city. It's 1,000 years old. And even the buildings-- even the house we lived in-- each house is built, you know, for 1,000 years to stay. It's got two and a half bricks, the front-- you got cross way, a brick between one room and the other. That's how strong those houses are built.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we saw that-- maybe a half hour later, we saw the first soldier. The way that's set up, one soldier came into the corner and he stopped. Next one come in, stops. This one goes left.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how they occupied the city. One block after the other. Each block-- one, forward. You know, with the-- they were maybe a little scared, maybe somebody is going to jump them, or whatever it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a beautiful day. What was it? The ninth month? September. September 6th was Krakow's occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Occupation was Wednesday. So it's only-- you got Saturday, Sunday, Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday. Yeah, fifth day they were here. They were ready--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you say that the cardinal went towards the German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Towards the Germans to bring them in, you know, to invite them, instead to destroy the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you saw this? You physically saw it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw it when they came. People were outside. We were-- you know, what it is, you know. But who cares? We want-- we want-- we want to see everything. You know, when you're 15 years old, you've got to see everything. You cannot just--

words.text: I saw it when they came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you realize that this was a sign of a problem, when you saw this? You did?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew--the sign of problems-- we knew, every time there was a speech on the radio, you know? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But where can you go if you don't have the means? Oh, sure. When my father would have had maybe a lot of money, there was the United States. There was the World Fair. He could have go himself. Maybe took my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would we do with the kids? Leave them with the maid, or whatever it is? It's not-- So when they came in you stayed in your home? When the Germans came in--

speaker: interviewee
text: They came in, we went outside. And we watched this, everything going on, you know. Then more and more. And about 4 o'clock in the afternoon, they came in already with their little-- they came with jeeps. And they had those short guns, you know-- with two wheels. But they're very short. When I say-- the Polacks had the nice one. Long ones from England and this-- But when they start coming there was no end to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: The amount?

speaker: interviewee
text: The amount, it's unbelievable. And then the third day, they came with trucks. We never saw trucks like this. You know, all diesel, big trucks, loaded. Supply-- I would say, we saw this. You know what? When you see this, and you see the Polish parade, it was like a-- 80 to one. It's unbelievable. The strength, the power. And then, when the city, the streets start thundering just from looking on how they come in, you know. The power, it's unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- what happened in your home, when you went home? How were your mother and father-- what were they saying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. They would say, well, [NON-ENGLISH], what are we going to do? Nothing. We can do nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And life-- from the beginning, life was going on as is. And it was right before Rosh Hashanah, then, you know, Yom Kippur, and everybody-- you know, it was still going on the same way, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we heard that they went into a shul, and people got scared, started running away. And they were laughing, and you know, that stuff. They were starting-- they start to feel it out, whatever-- you know, the way I understood.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were-- there were no Polish soldiers with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the Polacks had their own troubles. you know. They had to-- first of all, they were prisoners of war. They lost 3,000 soldiers in the war. That's the whole thing, you know? And they were lucky. They were-- if they would have more ammunition, more everything, they would suffer more. It's unbelievable, the strength of the Germans. The uniforms. He had a rifle, he had a packet gun, he had grenades. He had everything. The Polish soldier has 10 bullets. And I told them, there's eight soldiers against-- eight Germans against one Polish soldier. The last one, you use it for yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened after they came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came in, the army came in. And there was still, from the beginning, they think what was going on. You stay away, you know. Then you heard that they're cutting beards, and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you saw somebody with a beard, they said, listen, don't make a fool out of yourself. You want them to cut the beard? Cut your own. Who was cutting the beards?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German soldiers. They had fun, you know. From beginning, just like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: They would stop somebody on the streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: They'd stop on the street, you know, and they were laughing. Hey, what is this? Come on. They were talking, and the Jews would understand. You know-- it wasn't such a big difference from German to--

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw this? You saw somebody's beard being cut?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw it, but you know, it was-- from my point of view as a kid, 14, 15 years, we don't need the beard. You know? I knew great Hasidim, great everything is. The same thing with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother wouldn't go out. My father put on the [NON-ENGLISH] with the [NON-ENGLISH] and he schlep me to the-- to the shul. And I had to stay in the shul until [NON-ENGLISH], you have to get up. And he took the towels, around the [NON-ENGLISH], so he had to-- he couldn't hold me anymore. He needed both hands, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Out we went. You know, that's how it is. And we were already outside, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see little children-- the little children with the payots? Were they also--

speaker: interviewee
text: The little children with the payots, they-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, the word went out. He said look, why should I have troubles? Let's get rid of it. you know. And that's what happened. And they got-- they got the payots, they cut them off. I mean, you can be just as religious without the payots, or without.

speaker: interviewer
text: But after the payots were cut off, and after the beards were cut off, is it better?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, you know, then about two, three weeks later-- they start-- a lot of Jews left to Russia, and they still didn't come back, you know. And the store will close. You know, the stores with the materials-- with the buttons. I mean, you couldn't buy a button if you didn't go to one of those stores. And this was all Jewish stuff, you know. And the Germans start coming in, opening up the stores. And, you know, the robberies started. You should see-- when you're there, and when you-- when they go-- First they went in, and they took what they wanted, you know. Maybe they-- it was a-- this was hot items. You know, the-- the thread. Buttons are all right, it wasn't such-- and this-- they took whatever they wanted. They left the stores open. And then the crowd goes in, and start throwing out all this stuff. They load themselves up. They take whatever is left. And this goes on for twenty-- the stores were loaded. You know, there was a lot of merchandise. Everybody was loaded.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father's store also closed?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father close his store too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't have store.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had a factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a factory-- locked up everything. I mean it was-- 90% of our work, my father sent it up, you know? It came in in the season. Like the-- what do we need-- what are we going to do with all those Poles? Where are we going to keep them-- you know, pay them?

speaker: interviewer
text: So we pay as we went. The guy who made it said, OK. You keep it. You know, when the season comes in, the stuff came in, and it was packaged and sent out. So you're saying that many, many, many Jews--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're historically correct. Polish Jews left to the Soviet Union. I think they're saying--

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't the Soviet Union yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: They went-- they went-- the Germans on this side, the Russians on this side. So everybody went east. That side-- you know? They went east.

speaker: interviewer
text: And those stores that got closed because the Jews left, were they being looted by Germans, or by the Polish, or both?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Germans had to open up. The Polacks wouldn't open up. But what are the Germans going to do with it? They need a certain amount of something, they open up the store, and they have a lot of fun, you know-- and then the Polacks come in. Some of the stores-- they close in. You know, there was a store, you know, near our house where we used to live, the [NON-ENGLISH] 45. There was like a little mall, 11 stores. They called it the [NON-ENGLISH]. And there was-- there was like a sort of-- it was a jeweler's store, a perfume store. There were two shoe stores. There was a bra store. [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait a minute-- perfume. No-- and then oh, there was a store with button stuff like this. All kind of [NON-ENGLISH], they used to call it. Right? What happened to this mall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those stores-- you know, when came at night the housekeeper-- you know, from the house-- used to close it in the front. So-- and they opened up. And those stores-- I remember there was a store who had a lot of shoes, you know. This was a man, Silverstein. And he used to send out to make shoes. Those shoes were expert shoes-- they weren't shoes that my father would buy for me. They were too expensive. And he used to have-- let's say-- east of Krakow, he sent all the left shoes to make. They were-- they were making left shoes. The west of Krakow, they were making, you know, the right shoes. And every Friday, I remember there was like a line. They came in, and they got paid for the shoes. And they got new parts of leather that had the tops. And they give them a certain amount of thread, a certain amount of-- you know, altogether. That's how he did his business. And he was a friend of my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went and I say, I want shoes like this-- they were nice, there was only one stitch. It was just like you would say, I'm going to buy Bali shoes instead to buy, you know, any other cheap brand.

speaker: interviewee
text: And those shoes-- they came in. And then there was a woman-- he went to the Russian side. There was a woman, his wife, with three kids and they had no shoes to put on. And she was begging the Germans to give them some, you know. Let me in, it's my store. Give me, at least for the kids, the shoes. They wouldn't give it to them. And they used to come every week and pick up for their friends, you know. And send it into Germany, and everything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you realize that it wasn't only about the Jews who look like Hasidic Jews that this was about? Did it ever occur to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we could-- you could feel-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] getting tighter.

speaker: interviewer
text: The noose.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, you know, the noose gets tighter and tighter and tighter. All of a sudden comes in a couple trucks, and oh, we need somebody to unload a certain amount of cement. Then they just picking up people on the street. You're just-- let's say you go about your own business. You know, they used to do it in the morning. At 8, 9 o'clock in the morning, you know, and that's when the husband went to buy the bread and he doesn't come home. Then they dump them back at about 4 o'clock. You know, they started-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Somehow I always got away from beginning. Tell me about the ghetto. Was there a ghetto in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh sure. Later on was a ghetto. But first of all, the Jewish quarters-- they knew where it is. They used to come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: They needed four-- they needed to-- to help load, unload potatoes. Who knows? Whatever it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then starts to get-- no, before then, my father-- something happened and he said we had to get out from the house. And we pack up. Send out everything to different Polish people-- you know, split the equipment, split the materials. There was a lot of brass and copper. You know, Alpaca. You know, has to have fabricated stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was before the ghetto was established?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the ghetto--before the ghetto. And we went-- there was about, let's say, 10, 12 kilometers out of Krakow, you know-- the border. My father rented over there. There were some native Jews. You call it [NON-ENGLISH]-- you know, who lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Provincial Jews, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Provincial Jews. And they had-- they made a living out-- they used to buy off trees for the year. Fruit trees. This was a [NON-ENGLISH]. About the-- you know-- and that's when he came-- when they start ripening, they had to sleep there under the trees, you know, making sure that no-- since they paid for the fruit that nobody else picks it up, you know. And we met those guys-- know this Szal Lewiczi. He's now in-- I-- I see him every year, in Miami he lives. And he was here in the diamond center. Quite a rich man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lewiczi-- he could buy fruit. He could sell on 47th Street diamonds, and he did very good for himself. You know? So you went to live in that are--

speaker: interviewee
text: So he's-- somehow we got a better apartment than he had. You know, that Polish family. Eh, they had two horses, they had like four cows, and they had like six acres of land. I don't know more-- things that look bigger than an acre. Whatever it is. And they were nice to us. We paid-- we paid them good. And this was quite an experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did your family stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came in-- it was-- let's say February. And we stayed at-- I don't know where we stayed over the winter. Anyway, we came in 1940 there-- maybe we stayed until spring '41. Something like this, we stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then we moved on. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: One day-- I don't know what it was. Me starting up with the daughter-- whatever it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: The daughter of-- the daughter of a Polish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she was-- you know-- [LAUGHS] she was-- you know, something. I don't know what it was. Anyway, she came in to my mother, and she said she won't sell us any more food-- that we're going to have to take care ourselves to buy food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember, all right, the food start getting low. So my father-- my-- and I had a bicycle at that time. He said go up to Skawa and buy the food, you know? So I took the bicycle. It's seven kilometers to the town-- and I went out there, and there were some merchants. And I bought enough food.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the way back, when I'm going home-- yeah, this must be later already. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the way back-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't want-- I heard that the police is on the road, and I don't want them to take my bicycle. So I'm going with this [NON-ENGLISH]. How can I tell you? Between-- let's say here you've got potatoes, and here you've got wheat. So you have a line there So I was going between there. I took the long way, you know. That's-- I stay a quarter mile away, so in case-- I knew that they're standing there, so they're not going to bother me. And they came in a hail.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the only time, till today even, I never saw something like it. It started-- it start a little rain. You know, the sun came down very fast, and then came the hail. I-- I had the food. I didn't know what to do with it. Put it under my shirt, all over it. And he had a bicycle. I have nothing to cover up myself. And it's the middle of the summer. And I got so beaten up, you know-- that I cried. I was a little boy, what, 16 years old. I cried like a baby. And I was so beaten up. You know, from that hail.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the hail?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, from the hail. Then I come home, you know, with all the children-- I'm real-- I'm swollen. My head, you know? I never knew that hail can do so much. I'm coming home, my mother said. Quiet-- I want to blame this thing on someone else. Everything got damaged, you know. It was already soaked in, you know. I had barley, I had-- you know, beans and stuff like this. So she says, nothing. Why have I come in-- and maybe half an hour. That woman, the-- the Polish lady. They were--they were nice people, I cannot say. Then she start crying. They went out to the field, and all the wheat, you know, was almost-- it wasn't ready yet, you know-- got all beat up. And they lost the crop. And she came out. I remember like the day, you know-- how primitive they were. She went on her knees, and cried to my mother. She was holding her, as I say. That God punished her for her, with the big mouth, and she didn't want to sell us food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother said, come on. Why you believing in all that stuff? My mother had to quiet her down. Your mother comforted the Polish lady?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, you know, come on. Magda was her name. Why are you doing that stuff? This doesn't mean anything, you know. It just happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember-- what do I know about farming? Nothing. We went there, and we tried to straighten out whatever it was stood a little thing. But it was not-- there was no use, you know. So let's say you've got to grow like four feet, and it was only like two and a half. Less than three feet, and the-- and the seed was still small. But it was-- it wasn't so bad. And there was talking there's going to be a ghetto. And they're going to make it and probably building. So my father say, look, [NON-ENGLISH] Well, why should we have better than that news?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] And you had no electricity, and you have the lamb, and you have the outhouse with this everything. You know, when you live in the city, you have your shower, you have your comfort. We happen to have a very expensive and-- and a nice apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- your father wanted to go back, and join the other Jews--

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father said, let's come [NON-ENGLISH]. And we went in, and we got the papers to get into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Into the ghetto of Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we came to the ghetto, and there was a family there. And what they didn't-- what they didn't want them in the ghetto. I remember Roman Zukerman, they took away his mother and his uncle. And they had an apartment that was two bedrooms and a kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that he became like our-- my brother. He was my age, Roman Zukerman. So my mother cooked already.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- so we lived in that-- that apartment. The grand-- no. My aunt with the-- with the husband-- so we were four. There were six, And Roman, you know, the Roman Zukerman who--
